unit.name: Sampling-based decoding, truncation, temperature, and reranking
unit.description: Builds the main stochastic decoding toolbox for open-ended generation and explains how reranking can improve sampled outputs.
unit.summary: This segment argues that if maximum-probability decoding is wrong for open-ended text, decoding should introduce randomness by sampling from the next-token distribution [ts=1356s]. Vanilla sampling increases diversity but suffers from heavy-tailed vocabularies, where many individually low-probability bad tokens still have substantial aggregate mass [ts=1386s]. To cut the tail, top-k sampling restricts choices to the k highest-probability tokens, but the lecture shows fixed k can either remove valid options or leave implausible ones, creating recall and precision problems depending on the shape of the distribution [ts=1456s] [ts=1671s]. Nucleus or top-p sampling addresses this by sampling from the smallest candidate set whose cumulative probability exceeds p, giving an adaptive effective k [ts=1741s] [ts=1769s]. The segment also sketches typical sampling and epsilon sampling as other truncation families [ts=1875s]. Temperature scaling is presented as an orthogonal control: larger temperature flattens the distribution and increases diversity, while smaller temperature sharpens it and approaches greedy decoding [ts=2009s]. Finally, reranking is introduced: sample multiple candidates, score them with perplexity or richer objectives such as style, coherence, entailment, factuality, and consistency, and choose the best candidate; multiple rerankers can be composed [ts=2150s] [ts=2218s].

key_points.text: Sampling introduces randomness so the model no longer always follows the single highest-probability continuation.
Heavy-tailed token distributions make vanilla sampling risky because bad low-probability words have substantial aggregate probability mass.
Top-k sampling truncates the vocabulary to the k highest-probability tokens.
Top-p (nucleus) sampling uses cumulative probability mass, giving an adaptive effective k across different distributions.
Typical sampling and epsilon sampling are alternative truncation strategies based on entropy or minimum probability thresholds.
Temperature rescales logits: tau greater than 1 flattens the distribution, tau less than 1 sharpens it, and tau near 0 approximates greedy decoding.
Reranking samples multiple candidates and selects the one with the best quality score, potentially combining style and factuality objectives.

kp_catalog.name: Sampling-based decoding controls
kp_catalog.description: The family of stochastic decoding methods and hyperparameters such as top-k, top-p, epsilon, typical sampling, and temperature.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because sampling-based decoding controls clarifies the family of stochastic decoding methods and hyperparameters such as top-k, top-p, epsilon, typical sampling, and temperature, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Reranking sampled candidates
kp_catalog.description: Selecting among multiple sampled generations using auxiliary quality scores such as style, coherence, or factuality.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because reranking sampled candidates clarifies selecting among multiple sampled generations using auxiliary quality scores such as style, coherence, or factuality, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Degeneration under maximum-probability decoding
kp_catalog.description: The failure mode where greedy or beam-style search produces repetitive, overconfident text in open-ended generation.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because degeneration under maximum-probability decoding clarifies the failure mode where greedy or beam-style search produces repetitive, overconfident text in open-ended generation, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.
